title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a community theatre. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Rosa, The Costume Store Keeper
Rosa joined the amateur theatre group when she went to donate old curtains and found the costume room impossible to enter. Within a month, she was running it. [47]Her first challenge was arranging regular access to the wardrobe, because the key was kept by an actor who often arrived after rehearsals had already begun.[/47] People wasted time waiting in socks and half-finished outfits. [48]Her experience managing a clothes shop helped her organise rails by size, period and repair needs, and to track which items had been borrowed.[/48] Some performers missed the old chaos, but they admitted fewer buttons disappeared. Rosa says costumes taught her that confidence can hang on a small hook: when an actor finds the right jacket quickly, the rehearsal begins differently. She enjoys the quiet backstage moments when clothes stop looking like donations and start becoming characters. That transformation still makes her smile after rehearsals. every week.
texts.B:
### Liam, The Youth Workshop Assistant
[49]Liam became involved after the director of the theatre’s youth group, who had taught him years earlier, asked him to support Saturday workshops.[/49] He had loved acting as a teenager, but helping others perform felt more demanding. [50]He was surprised by the safeguarding paperwork and permission checks required before young actors could rehearse scenes, use props or be photographed.[/50] At first, the formalities seemed far removed from creativity. Then he saw how safe structures allowed shy participants to take risks on stage. Liam’s best memory is of a boy who refused to speak during warm-ups but later improvised an entire scene as a detective. Liam learned to praise effort without making anyone feel exposed. He says youth theatre is not about producing polished actors, but about giving young people a room where trying something strange feels possible. He now prepares every session with patience, humour and spare plans. each time.
texts.C:
### Julia, The Foyer Display Designer
Julia, a photographer, offered to help after repeatedly waiting in the theatre foyer while her partner rehearsed. [51]During those waits, she noticed visitors staring at a blank wall instead of learning anything about the productions or the people behind them.[/51] She suggested creating changing displays with rehearsal photos and short captions. [55]Her captions used clear, lively language so people could understand each picture quickly without needing theatre knowledge.[/55] [52]The worst moment came when a display board collapsed during a busy interval and scattered photographs across the floor.[/52] Julia felt mortified, but several audience members helped pick everything up and asked questions about the show. She remounted the display more securely the next day. Julia says the foyer now feels like part of the performance rather than a waiting area. It gives nervous newcomers something to look at before they decide where to sit. That sense of welcome pleased her. deeply.
texts.D:
### Ethan, The Relaxed Performance Helper
[53]Ethan volunteered because his younger cousin, who has sensory sensitivities, loved stories but avoided ordinary theatre performances.[/53] He wanted the group to offer relaxed performances where audience members could move, make noise or leave briefly without embarrassment. [54]His hardest task was coordinating door staff, lighting changes and sound checks so adjustments happened smoothly without distracting actors or unsettling families.[/54] He learned that a small warning before a loud effect could transform an evening. Some older members worried the changes would spoil the show, but Ethan invited them to observe a rehearsal and discuss concerns. He was proud when his cousin stayed until the final scene. [56]He is now training a small access team so relaxed performances can continue without depending on his personal connection to the issue.[/56] Ethan says theatre should not ask people to hide who they are in order to attend. The result felt generous rather than disruptive.

questions.question: mentions rehearsals being delayed because access to costumes depended on one person?
questions.explanation: Rosa says the wardrobe key was kept by an actor who often arrived late.

questions.question: used retail experience to organise clothes and track borrowed items?
questions.explanation: Rosa used her clothes-shop experience to organise rails and track borrowed costumes.

questions.question: was invited by someone who had previously taught them?
questions.explanation: Liam joined after his former teacher asked him to support youth workshops.

questions.question: was surprised by formal checks before young people could take part in creative activities?
questions.explanation: Liam was surprised by safeguarding paperwork and permission checks for rehearsals, props and photographs.

questions.question: noticed an unused display opportunity while waiting during rehearsals?
questions.explanation: Julia noticed visitors staring at a blank wall while she waited in the foyer.

questions.question: felt embarrassed when finished display material fell apart in public?
questions.explanation: Julia’s display board collapsed and scattered photographs during an interval.

questions.question: wanted to improve performances because a relative avoided ordinary theatre settings?
questions.explanation: Ethan’s cousin has sensory sensitivities and avoided ordinary performances.

questions.question: had to coordinate practical adjustments without distracting performers or audiences?
questions.explanation: Ethan coordinated door staff, lighting changes and sound checks smoothly.

questions.question: wrote captions that helped people understand images without specialist knowledge?
questions.explanation: Julia’s captions used clear language that did not require theatre knowledge.

questions.question: is training a team so accessible performances do not depend on their personal link to the issue?
questions.explanation: Ethan is training an access team so relaxed performances can continue.
